perturbation.original_text: 1. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION “Confidential Information” means collectively and individually all or any information of whatsoever nature, di sclosed in any form, whether oral, visual, written or in electronic form (including, without limitation, any documentation, reports, calculations, processes, methodologies, manuals and membership data ) relating to [Company name] and FORS operators, audit processes and commercial data together with all records or copies or e xtracts thereof in whatever media held.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION “Confidential Information” means information disclosed in any form, whether oral or written, relating to [Company name] and FORS operators.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' has been significantly narrowed. Specifically, the exclusion of 'visual, written or in electronic form (including, without limitation, any documentation, reports, calculations, processes, methodologies, manuals and membership data )' creates a direct contradiction later in the document as the term is referenced in broader contexts where it would normally encompass documents, reports, and electronic data. This particularly affects section 5.1.1 which refers to making copies of Confidential Information, thus creating uncertainty regarding what can be copied, since by removing that part of the confidential information, there are no documents, or records to copy.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1.1 The r eceiving Party may only make such copies of Confidential Information as are expressly authorised by the disclosing Party for the Permitted Purpose and for disclosures that are not in breach of this Agreement. The receiving Party will clearly label all copies of Confidential Information as confidential.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing 'visual, written or in electronic form (including, without limitation, any documentation, reports, calculations, processes, methodologies, manuals and membership data ).' This creates a direct contradiction with section 5.1.1, which allows the receiving party to make copies of Confidential Information. Since the changed definition no longer includes documents or electronic data, it's unclear what the receiving party is authorized to copy, rendering the clause in section 5.1.1 uncertain and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 3. PERMITTED DISCLOSURE 3.1 The receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information: 3.1.1 only to such employees of the receiving Party as are strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose; and/or 3.1.2 as required by any order of any court of competent jurisdiction or any competent judicial, governmental or regularly body. 3.2 Prior to disclosure of any Confidential I nformation under Clause 3.1.2, the r eceiving Party shall (to the extent permitt ed by law) inform the disclosing Party of the complete circumstances and the details of the information to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. PERMITTED DISCLOSURE 3.1 The receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information: 3.1.1 only to such employees of the receiving Party as are strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose; and/or 3.1.2 as required by any order of any court of competent jurisdiction or any competent judicial, governmental or regularly body.
perturbation.explanation: Clause 3.2 which requires the receiving party to inform the disclosing party prior to disclosure as required by law has been removed. This creates a contradiction since the receiving party is still bound by 3.1.2 to disclose information if required by law or court order, however the obligation to inform the disclosing party before doing so is removed. This removal can have implications on section 6.1, in particular on the first party indemnity when the disclosing party doesn't have knowledge of the disclosure that has taken place in time. Also, this creates a contradiction, and enforcement of the clause becomes uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1 Each Party shall indemnify and hol d harmless the other Party against all loss, damage and expense (including legal expenses) whatsoever and howsoever arising out of or in connection with any breach by the first Party or its employees of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The removal of clause 3.2, which required the receiving party to inform the disclosing party prior to disclosing confidential information as required by law or court order (3.1.2), creates a contradiction with section 6.1. Section 6.1 states that each party shall indemnify and hold harmless the other party against any breach of the agreement. Without clause 3.2, the disclosing party may not be aware of the disclosure, potentially hindering their ability to prevent or mitigate damages. This directly impacts the indemnity provision in 6.1, as the disclosing party's awareness (or lack thereof due to the omission) affects the 'loss, damage and expense' arising from a breach of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 4. THIRD PARTIES 4.1 The receiving Party shall notify anyone to whom it is authorised to disclose the Confidential Information that it is confidential and procure that they comply with this Agreement. On the request of the d isclosing Party, the r eceiving Party shall procure that they enter into a confidentiality agreement on terms and conditions no less onerous than those contained in this Agreement. 4.2 Neither P arty may assign this Agreement without the prior written consent of the other Party . 4.3 This Agreement shall not confer and shall not purport to confer on any third party any benefit or any right to enforce any term of this Agreement for the purposes of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. THIRD PARTIES 4.2 Neither P arty may assign this Agreement without the prior written consent of the other Party . 4.3 This Agreement shall not confer and shall not purport to confer on any third party any benefit or any right to enforce any term of this Agreement for the purposes of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Clause 4.1 has been removed. This clause requires the receiving party to notify anyone they disclose confidential information to, that the information is confidential and to ensure they comply with the agreement. By removing this, Section 3.1 now contradicts Clause 4.2. The receiving party is still allowed to disclose confidential information to employees necessary for the Permitted Purpose, but is no longer required to inform them of the confidentiality or make them comply with the NDA. This undermines the purpose of having an NDA and introduces uncertainty in enforcement, and creates a direct in text contradiction with 3.1.1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.  PERMITTED DISCLOSURE 3.1 The receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information: 3.1.1 only to such employees of the receiving Party as are strictly necessary for the Permitted Purpose; and/or 3.1.2 as required by any order of any court of competent jurisdiction or any competent judicial, governmental or regularly body.
perturbation.justification: The removal of clause 4.1, which obligated the receiving party to ensure confidentiality of disclosed information to third parties, directly contradicts section 3.1.1. Section 3.1.1 allows disclosure to employees for the Permitted Purpose, but without 4.1, there's no explicit requirement for those employees to be bound by confidentiality. This creates a clear contradiction by undermining the confidentiality obligations outlined elsewhere in the contract, as those employees are not bound to complying to the NDA.

perturbation.original_text: 5. COPIES 5.1 The receiving Party shall acquire no proprietary interest in or right to the Confidential Information supplied to it by the disclosing Party . 5.1.1 The r eceiving Party may only make such copies of Confidential Information as are expressly authorised by the disclosing Party for the Permitted Purpose and for disclosures that are not in breach of this Agreement. The receiving Party will clearly label all copies of Confidential Information as confidential. 5.2 If the disclosing Party so requests in writing, the r eceiving Party shall immediately: - 5.2.1 return all Confidential Information including copies to the disclosing Party ; and 5.2.2 destroy or permanently erase all copies made by the receiving Party and procure that anyone to whom the receiving Party ha s supplied copies destroys or permanently erases such copies and any further copies made by them.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. COPIES 5.1 The receiving Party shall acquire no proprietary interest in or right to the Confidential Information supplied to it by the disclosing Party . 5.2 If the disclosing Party so requests in writing, the r eceiving Party shall immediately: - 5.2.1 return all Confidential Information including copies to the disclosing Party ; and 5.2.2 destroy or permanently erase all copies made by the receiving Party and procure that anyone to whom the receiving Party ha s supplied copies destroys or permanently erases such copies and any further copies made by them.
perturbation.explanation: Clause 5.1.1 has been removed. This clause outlines the conditions under which the receiving party can make copies of confidential information. With this clause removed, the receiving party is no longer explicitly authorized to make copies for the Permitted Purpose. However, clause 5.2 still requires the receiving party to return or destroy 'all copies' upon request. This creates a contradiction, as there's no explicit permission to create the copies in the first place. The uncertainty lies in whether creating copies is implicitly allowed, and if so, under what conditions. Without clause 5.1.1, enforcement is questionable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.2 If the disclosing Party so requests in writing, the r eceiving Party shall immediately: - 5.2.1 return all Confidential Information including copies to the disclosing Party ; and 5.2.2 destroy or permanently erase all copies made by the receiving Party and procure that anyone to whom the receiving Party ha s supplied copies destroys or permanently erases such copies and any further copies made by them.
perturbation.justification: The omission of clause 5.1.1 in the 'Changed Text' removes the explicit authorization for the receiving party to make copies of Confidential Information. However, clause 5.2, which is also the 'Contradicted Text', still requires the receiving party to return or destroy 'all copies'. This creates a direct contradiction because the receiving party is obligated to return or destroy copies, but there is no longer explicit permission to create them in the first place. This satisfies the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' as essential language has been removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 6. GENERAL 6.1 Each Party shall indemnify and hol d harmless the other Party against all loss, damage and expense (including legal expenses) whatsoever and howsoever arising out of or in connection with any breach by the first Party or its employees of this Agreement. 6.2 Each Party acknowledges and agrees that damages alone may not be an adequate remedy for a breach of this Agreement and that each Party shall be entitled to an order for injunctive relief in the event of any actual, threatened or anticipated breach of this Agreement by the other Party . 6.3 Nothing in this Agreement or its operation shall constitute an obligation on either Party to enter into any proposed joint venture, consortium or partnership or pursue any commercial arrangement with the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. GENERAL 6.2 Each Party acknowledges and agrees that damages alone may not be an adequate remedy for a breach of this Agreement and that each Party shall be entitled to an order for injunctive relief in the event of any actual, threatened or anticipated breach of this Agreement by the other Party . 6.3 Nothing in this Agreement or its operation shall constitute an obligation on either Party to enter into any proposed joint venture, consortium or partnership or pursue any commercial arrangement with the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing clause 6.1, the indemnification clause, while the agreement anticipates and provides for injunctive relief (6.2), it eliminates the explicit provision for indemnification against losses, damages, and expenses arising from breaches. This creates a contradiction, as the agreement still acknowledges potential breaches and offers a remedy (injunctive relief), however does not provide a remedy for damages due to the breach. This omission undermines the agreement's intent to protect each party from financial harm resulting from a breach, and potentially contradicts the spirit of the agreement. Also, by removing the indemnification clause 2.1.1, 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 might be affected since the indemnity is being removed, making the confidentiality requirements weaker
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1.1 to keep the Confidential Information secret and acknowledges the proprietary nature of the Confidential Information;
perturbation.justification: The removal of the indemnification clause (6.1) in the 'Changed Text' creates a contradiction with clause 2.1.1 in the 'Contradicted Text', as the removal of the indemnification weakens the protection of Confidential Information, meaning that if one party breaches the confidentiality agreement, the other party might not have full protection against losses or damages, which contradicts the spirit and intent of maintaining the confidentiality. The explanation notes the impact of indemnity removal on confidentiality requirements, and the change fits the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' as it removes essential language that creates a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 7. GOVERNING LAW 7.1 No amendments, changes or modifications to this Agreement shall be valid except if the same are agreed in writing and signed by a duly authorised representative or each of the P arties hereto. 7.2 This Agreement comprises the full and complete agreement of the Parties her eto with respect to the Confidential Information and supersedes and cancels all prior communications, understandings and agreements between the P arties relating to the Confidential Information whether oral, express or implied. 7.3 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the Law s of England and Wales . Each Party irrevocably agrees to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the C ourts of England to settle any dispute that aris es out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. GOVERNING LAW 7.1 No amendments, changes or modifications to this Agreement shall be valid except if the same are agreed in writing and signed by a duly authorised representative or each of the P arties hereto. 7.3 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the Law s of England and Wales . Each Party irrevocably agrees to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the C ourts of England to settle any dispute that aris es out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing clause 7.2, we eliminate the 'entire agreement' clause. This clause stated that the agreement represents the full understanding between the parties, superseding any prior communications. Its removal creates uncertainty, as it now becomes unclear whether prior agreements or understandings could still be relevant. This affects Section 1 (Confidential Information), since it could now be argued that the definition of 'Confidential Information' is not solely defined within this agreement but could be influenced by prior communications, contradicting the intent of Section 1. This is a subtle, but potentially significant, in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION “Confidential Information” means information disclosed in any form, whether oral or written, relating to [Company name] and FORS operators.
perturbation.justification: The original text contained clause 7.2, which explicitly stated the agreement was the 'full and complete agreement' and superseded prior communications. This provided certainty regarding the scope of 'Confidential Information' defined in Section 1. By removing clause 7.2, the changed text creates ambiguity. It becomes unclear whether prior understandings or communications could influence the definition of 'Confidential Information', directly contradicting Section 1's implicit assumption that the definition is solely within the agreement. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition, where essential language is removed, creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.
